EMN1.conclusion: ln(x) is NOT exactly EMN-representable. Every EMN tree has a nonzero exp(·) residual. Best approximation approaches ln(x) as depth increases but never reaches it.
EMN1.note_floating_point_trap: N=8 tree achieves IEEE 754 MSE=0 by driving L(x) to ~-1.6e25, causing exp(L) to underflow to 0.0 in double precision. In exact arithmetic the residual exp(L) is nonzero (~1e-{1.6e25}). The structural proof is correct.

EMN3.theorem: ln(x) is not exactly EMN-representable (real or complex)
EMN3.proof_method: Growth rate: R(x) = x·exp(exp(L)) cannot be an EMN tree (infinite regress)
EMN3.complex_case: Same argument — exp(z) ≠ 0 for all z ∈ ℂ
EMN3.status: Proved (growth rate lemma assumes bounded tower growth, which holds by structural induction)

EMN4.mechanism: Uses complex intermediates; both ln and exp approximable to arbitrary precision
EMN4.tree_size_bound: O(d * log log(1/eps)) for d-level EML function at precision eps
EMN4.theorem: EMN is approximately complete: for any elementary function f, compact interval [a,b], and eps>0, there exists an EMN tree T (using complex intermediates) with |Re(T(x))-f(x)| < eps on [a,b].
